cues.x: Getting to Yes, by Roger Fisher, William Ury, and Bruce Patton.
Wherever you are right now, driving, walking on a treadmill, folding laundry, I want you to think back to the last time you argued with somebody over something that actually mattered.
Maybe it was a raise.
Maybe it was a contractor who overcharged you, or a sibling fighting over what to do with a parent's house, or a landlord who wouldn't fix the heat.
However small or large it was, you were negotiating, whether you called it that or not.
And the book we're getting into today is, without much competition, the single most influential book ever written on how to do that well.

It's called Getting to Yes, Negotiating Agreement Without Giving In, written by Roger Fisher and William Ury, with Bruce Patton joining as a co-author in later editions, all three of them tied to the Harvard Negotiation Project, which Fisher helped establish at Harvard Law School.
It first came out in 1981, and it did something almost no book manages to do.

It took a skill people assumed you were simply born with or not, the fast talking salesman, the silver tongued lawyer, and turned it into something you could study, break into parts, and get better at on purpose.

It has sold millions of copies, been translated into dozens of languages, and if you've ever sat through a negotiation class in a business school, a law school, or a diplomatic training program, you were almost certainly taught some version of what's in these pages, even if nobody told you where it originally came from.

Now, if you've already gone through Never Split the Difference in this library, the one by Chris Voss, the former FBI hostage negotiator, keep that book in the back of your mind for a second, because this is its parent.

Voss pushed back hard against parts of this approach, arguing that real people in real crises are far more emotional and far less rational than this book sometimes assumes, and that's a fair challenge.
But Getting to Yes came first, by more than two decades, and it's the foundation that everything after it, Voss included, has had to answer to.

Where Voss is a former federal agent walking you through hostage standoffs, Fisher, Ury, and Patton are Harvard scholars walking you through everyday human conflict, business deals, divorces, border disputes, labor strikes, and plenty of situations where nobody is holding a gun and everybody could, in theory, just be reasonable, if only they knew how.
Think of this one as the calmer, older, more collaborative sibling.
It reads less like hostage rescue and more like diplomacy.
And once you actually learn it, you will start seeing it working, or conspicuously failing to work, everywhere you look, in your own kitchen, at your own job, in the news.
So let's start where the book starts, with the way almost everybody negotiates by default, something Fisher and Ury call positional bargaining.

Positional bargaining is what happens when two sides each pick a position, a number, a demand, a line drawn in the sand, and then push back and forth trying to drag the other side toward their end of that line.

You want three hundred thousand dollars for your house, the buyer offers two hundred fifty, you counter at two hundred ninety, they counter at two hundred sixty, and on it goes, each of you defending a number instead of ever asking what you actually need underneath it.
It feels like negotiating.
It even feels normal, because it's how most of us were raised to do this, from flea markets to used car lots to every sitcom negotiation you've ever watched.

But Fisher and Ury spend the opening pages of this book taking that instinct apart, because positional bargaining is quietly terrible at producing good outcomes, and it fails in one of two directions.
You can be soft about it, or you can be hard about it, and the book is precise that both of them fail, just differently.

The soft bargainer treats the other side like a friend, avoids conflict at almost any cost, and makes concessions readily to protect the relationship, often walking away having given up far more than they should have, simply because they wanted to be liked or wanted the discomfort to end.
That's soft on the people and soft on the problem, and the danger is obvious.
You protect the relationship and quietly gut your own interests.
The hard bargainer goes the opposite direction entirely, treats the other side as an adversary, digs in, makes extreme demands, uses threats, and treats every inch conceded as a personal loss.
That's hard on the people and hard on the problem, and it does win sometimes, but it wins ugly.

It turns the whole exchange into a contest of wills, it exhausts everyone involved, and it very often damages or destroys a relationship you actually needed to keep intact, a marriage, a business partnership, a colleague you will still see on Monday morning.

And here's the trap hiding inside all of it: soft bargainers who meet hard bargainers usually lose the position and something bigger besides, because a person who walks away feeling steamrolled does not forget it.
Soft and hard are still the same broken game.
They're just being played at different volumes.
You can see this same broken game at the tiniest scale and at the largest scale, and I want to plant two images in your head right now, because we are absolutely coming back to both of them.
At the tiny end, picture two coworkers sharing an office, arguing over a window.
One wants it open, the other wants it shut.
So what do they do?
They negotiate positions.
Open.
Shut.
Maybe they compromise on a crack of a few inches, which, predictably, leaves both of them unhappy, one still a little cold, one still a little stuffy.
At the enormous end, picture Israel and Egypt after 1967, the year Israeli forces captured the entire Sinai Peninsula, that huge stretch of desert sitting between the two countries.
For more than a decade afterward, the two nations essentially fought that same window argument on a map.
Israel's position, in various forms over the years, was that it needed to hold onto some or all of that land.
Egypt's position was that it wanted every last inch of the Sinai returned, full stop, non negotiable.
Two positions, planted at opposite ends of the same territory, and for years nobody could find a line that both sides would actually sign.
That's positional bargaining at the scale of nations, and it nearly kept two countries locked in hostility indefinitely.
Hold onto both of those pictures, the window and the Sinai, because in a few minutes this book is going to blow both of them wide open.
So if positional bargaining fails whether you play it soft or hard, what's the alternative?
This is really the whole point of the book.
Fisher and Ury propose a completely different method, which they call principled negotiation, or negotiating on the merits.
It rests on four points, four moves you make in essentially every negotiation you'll ever have, personal or professional, tiny or enormous.
Separate the people from the problem.
Focus on interests, not positions.
Invent options for mutual gain before you settle on anything.
And insist on using objective criteria whenever interests genuinely collide.
Let's walk through all four, slowly, because each one is going to change how you handle your next hard conversation.
First, separate the people from the problem.
That sounds almost too simple to matter, so let's get specific about what it actually means.
Every negotiator, no matter how professional, no matter how much authority they carry into the room, is a human being first.
They have emotions.
They have egos that bruise easily.

They have their own way of seeing the situation, shaped by their history, their fears, their incentives, a way of seeing that can be wildly different from yours even when you're both staring at the exact same facts.

Here's the trap that catches nearly everyone: in the middle of a disagreement, it is incredibly easy for the substance of the problem to get tangled up with the relationship between the people arguing about it.
You make a sharp comment about someone's proposal, and they hear it as a comment about their competence, or their character.
They push back hard on your number, and you hear it as disrespect.
Before long you're no longer negotiating a price, you're defending your ego, and the actual problem you both showed up to solve gets buried under two people quietly trying not to lose face.
The fix the book offers is a mental picture worth actually holding onto.

Instead of sitting across the table glaring at each other with the problem wedged awkwardly between you, picture both of you moving to the same side of the table, shoulder to shoulder, looking outward at the problem laid out in front of you like a puzzle you're solving together.
You and they, together, are working against the problem, not against each other.

Practically, this means dealing seriously with perception, genuinely trying to understand how the situation looks from inside the other person's head rather than from inside your own, because people act on their perception of reality, not on reality itself, and you will never move someone by arguing against a perception you haven't bothered to understand.

It means dealing openly with emotion, yours and theirs, letting people vent without treating it as a personal attack, acknowledging feelings as legitimate even when you disagree with the conclusions somebody draws from them.

And it means getting serious about communication, actually listening to understand rather than listening just long enough to plan your rebuttal, speaking about your own perspective and needs instead of making accusations about their intentions.
Do this well and you can disagree furiously about the problem while still respecting, even liking, the person on the other side of it.

Skip it, and you'll find that even a generous, fair offer gets rejected, not because of the numbers, but because somewhere along the way you made the other person feel small, and now they would rather lose money than hand you the satisfaction of a win.
Second, and this is really the heart of the entire book, focus on interests, not positions.
Get this distinction into your bones, because almost everything else builds on top of it.
A position is what someone says they want.
It's the number, the demand, the stated outcome.
An interest is why they want it, the underlying need, fear, hope, or concern that the position is just one possible way of satisfying.
Here's the insight that makes all of this so powerful: behind opposed positions, there are almost always more shared and compatible interests than conflicting ones.
You just can't see them yet, because both sides are standing there loudly announcing positions instead of explaining reasons.
Remember that window I asked you to hold onto?
Here's the full story, and it's one of the most famous small parables in all of negotiation literature, because it captures the whole idea in about thirty seconds.
Two people are in a room, arguing over a window.
One wants it open, the other wants it shut.
They go back and forth about how far open it should be, a crack, halfway, and neither one is satisfied with any version of that compromise.
Eventually a third person walks in, a librarian in the book's telling, and instead of proposing her own compromise on how far the window should open, she simply asks each of them a question.
She turns to the one who wants it open and asks why.
He says he wants some fresh air.
She turns to the one who wants it shut and asks why.
She says she doesn't want the draft blowing directly on her.
The librarian thinks about it for a second, walks into the next room, and opens a window wide in there instead.
Fresh air moves through the space.
No draft hits anyone directly.

Both people get exactly what they actually wanted, not sixty percent of it, not a grudging split, all of it, because their positions, open versus shut, were in direct conflict, but their interests, fresh air and no draft, were never in conflict at all.
Nobody finds that solution by arguing about the window itself.
You only find it by asking why.
Now let's go back to the Sinai, because it's the example Fisher and Ury lean on hardest, and it happens to be a genuinely remarkable piece of real history.
By the late nineteen seventies, Israel and Egypt had been stuck for years over what to do with that desert peninsula Israel had captured in 1967.

On the surface it looked like the most zero sum dispute imaginable, two countries wanting the same land, and land, unlike almost anything else you might negotiate over, cannot simply be split down the middle and shared without somebody literally drawing a border through it.

If you only look at positions, this is unsolvable, because Israel's position was some version of keeping the territory, and Egypt's position was recovering every single inch of it, and those two positions cannot both be true about the same acre of desert at the same time.

But in September of 1978, at Camp David, the presidential retreat in Maryland, with President Jimmy Carter mediating between Israel's Prime Minister Menachem Begin and Egypt's President Anwar Sadat, the negotiators finally did what nobody had managed in a decade of failed talks.
They stopped asking what each side would settle for and started asking why.
Why did Israel want to hold the Sinai?
Not because Israelis were desperate for more desert to live in.

They wanted it because of security, because as long as Egyptian tanks and airfields could sit right up against the border, Israel felt exposed to another surprise invasion like the ones it had already survived.
And why did Egypt insist on every inch back?
Not because that specific sand carried some enormous practical value.
It was about sovereignty, about national dignity, about the principle that not one grain of Egyptian territory should remain under someone else's flag.
Once you set those two interests side by side, security for Israel, sovereignty for Egypt, you can see instantly that they don't actually conflict at all.
A country can hold complete sovereignty over land without stationing an army on it.
So that's exactly what got built.
The agreement returned the entire Sinai Peninsula to Egypt, the Egyptian flag flying over every inch of it, full sovereignty, Egypt's interest, completely satisfied.

And large parts of it, especially the areas closest to the Israeli border, were demilitarized, no tanks, no offensive buildup, with international monitoring to enforce it, Israel's interest in security, just as completely satisfied.
Both original positions, Israel keeps it, Egypt gets it all back untouched, were abandoned entirely.
And both sides walked away with more than either position could ever have delivered on its own.
That's not a compromise.

A compromise would have meant splitting the peninsula down the middle, and it would have left Israel less secure and Egypt less sovereign, an outcome worse for both sides than the one they actually reached.
That's the power of digging past positions into interests, and it's why this example anchors the whole book.
There's a smaller, homelier version of the same idea that the book uses, and I suspect it might stick with you even longer than Camp David, because you can picture it happening in your own kitchen.
Two sisters are standing there, and there's exactly one orange left.
Both of them say they want it.
Stop right there, at the position, and it looks like a simple fifty fifty problem, so let's say they do the fair, grown up thing and cut the orange in half.
Each sister gets fifty percent of an orange.
Compromise reached, nobody thrilled, nobody furious.
Except here's the twist.
If either sister had bothered to ask the other why she wanted it, she would have discovered something remarkable.
One sister wanted the orange to eat, just the fruit, as a snack.
The other wanted it for a recipe, she needed the zest, the peel, to bake into a cake, and she was planning on throwing the actual fruit away entirely.
Their positions, I want the orange, I want the orange, were identical and completely at odds.
Their interests, eat the fruit, use the peel, were completely compatible, not even slightly in conflict.

Had they asked why before reaching for the knife, one sister could have had the entire peel and the other could have had the entire fruit, both of them getting a hundred percent of what they actually needed, instead of both getting a disappointing fifty percent of something neither of them fully wanted.
That's the quiet cost of stopping at positions.
You don't just risk losing.
You risk both sides losing, gently, while still calling it a fair deal.
So how do you actually go dig up interests, yours and theirs, in a real negotiation rather than a parable?
The most direct tool is the simplest question in the language, ask why.
Why do you want that number.
Why does that deadline matter to you.
Why is that particular clause a dealbreaker.
And ask it of your own position too, because you might discover that your own stated position isn't serving your own deeper interest either.

A second tool is asking why not, meaning, why haven't they already agreed to the obvious alternative you're about to propose, what's stopping them, because their answer usually reveals a concern you hadn't accounted for.

And it helps enormously to remember that most people, underneath whatever specific demand they're making, are chasing a fairly small set of basic human interests, security, economic wellbeing, a sense of belonging, recognition, and control over their own life.
When a negotiation feels stuck and irrational, it's very often because one of those basic needs is quietly driving the whole thing and nobody has said it out loud yet.
Third point, invent options for mutual gain, and generate real ones before you settle on anything.
Once you know the real interests on both sides, the tempting next move is to jump straight to a solution, usually the first one that comes to mind.
Fisher and Ury argue this is almost always a mistake, because most people's creative thinking gets strangled by four specific habits before it ever gets a chance to breathe.
The first is premature judgment, criticizing every idea the second it's spoken, which teaches everyone in the room, including you, to stop offering ideas at all.

The second is searching for the single right answer too early, hunting for the one correct solution from the very first minute instead of first generating a wide field of possibilities to choose from later.

The third is assuming the pie is fixed, treating the negotiation as though there's only one quantity of value in the room and whatever you gain, they must lose, when in reality the size of what's on the table can often be expanded before anyone divides it up.

And the fourth, this one is sneaky, is assuming that solving their side of the problem is their job, not yours, when actually a solution that also genuinely works for the other side is far more likely to get agreed to, and far more likely to actually hold up once everyone leaves the room.
The book's fix for all four traps is to physically and mentally separate the act of inventing options from the act of deciding on one.
In practice that means running something like a brainstorm, a stretch of time where the only rule is that no idea gets evaluated, criticized, or committed to.
You just generate as many possible options as you can, including the ones that sound a little wild, because a slightly ridiculous idea often contains the seed of the actual answer once you refine it.
Only after you've got a good long list on the table do you switch modes and start evaluating, narrowing, combining ideas together, and selecting.

Try to invent and decide at the same time and you strangle both processes, you judge every raw idea too harshly to let it develop, and you never feel finished generating because you keep second guessing whatever's already sitting on the table.

And while you're inventing, actively look for ways to expand the pie before you divide it, meaning, look at the differences between you and the other side, differences in what you each value, in your tolerance for risk, in your timeline, in your forecasts about the future, and use those differences to create value instead of fighting over it.

If they need cash quickly and you're comfortable waiting for a bigger payout over time, that single difference can build a deal structure that leaves both of you better off than a straight price negotiation ever would.
Great options rarely get found lying on the table already formed.
They get built, deliberately, by people who agreed to stop judging long enough to actually think.
Fourth point, insist on using objective criteria.
Here's an honest caveat the book is careful to include, and you should be too.
Sometimes, even after you've dug all the way down to real interests and invented a wide field of creative options, you still hit a point where interests genuinely, directly conflict.
There's only one house.
There's only one final price.
Somebody's interest in paying less and somebody's interest in receiving more cannot both be maximized on the same dollar.

When that happens, you're left with a choice about how to resolve it, and Fisher and Ury argue there are really only two ways to settle a genuine, head on collision of interests, a contest of wills, where whoever is more stubborn or more willing to walk away simply wins, or an appeal to some fair standard that exists independently of what either of you personally wants.
They want you to always choose the second one.
Objective criteria means fair, independent, legitimate standards that neither side invented on the spot to serve themselves.
Market value, what comparable properties, or comparable salaries, or comparable deals actually sold for.
Expert opinion, an independent appraiser, an engineer, an accountant, someone with no stake in who wins.
Precedent, what's been done in similar situations before, what the actual industry standard is.

Or, when all else fails, what a court or an arbitrator would likely decide if the dispute went to formal litigation, since that gives you a realistic, grounded benchmark for what's actually reasonable rather than what either of you simply wishes were true.
The genius of insisting on objective criteria is what it does to the emotional shape of a negotiation.
Nobody has to lose face by caving to the other person's raw willpower.
You're not giving in to them, and they're not giving in to you, you're both simply agreeing to be reasonable people who defer to a fair yardstick.

That preserves the relationship, it usually produces a wiser and more durable agreement, because a genuinely fair outcome tends to actually stick, and it's dramatically more efficient than a stubbornness contest that can drag on for weeks while both sides wait to see who blinks first.
Practically, this means when you're negotiating a salary, you don't just say you want more, you bring survey data on what the role actually pays in your market.
When you're negotiating a house price, you don't just say it's worth less, you bring three comparable sales.
You make the criteria the thing you're both examining side by side, rather than making each other the thing you're fighting.
Now we need to talk about the single most quoted concept to come out of this entire book, an idea so useful it has become permanent vocabulary in business schools and boardrooms everywhere.
BATNA, which stands for Best Alternative To a Negotiated Agreement.
Your BATNA is simply the answer to this question, if this negotiation completely falls apart right now, what will you actually do instead?
Not what you'd like to happen, what you would genuinely, realistically do.
If you're negotiating a job offer, your BATNA might be another offer already in hand, or staying in your current role, or taking three months to freelance while you keep looking.
If you're selling a car, your BATNA might be a different buyer who already made an offer, or simply keeping the car another year.
Here is the point Fisher and Ury want burned into your memory.
Your BATNA, not your size, not your confidence, not how badly you want this, not even how much money or leverage you appear to have, is the real source of your power in any negotiation.
Think about why.
If your alternative is fantastic, you can walk away from the table completely calm, almost indifferent, because you already know you'll land somewhere good either way.
That calm is not an act, and the other side can feel it, and it changes how they treat you.

If your alternative is terrible, you'll feel it in your body, you'll rush, you'll fold early, you'll accept terms you shouldn't, no matter how good you are at every other technique in this book, because desperation leaks out no matter how well you try to hide it.
So the highest leverage work you can do before any important negotiation isn't rehearsing clever lines, it's going out and actually improving your BATNA.
Line up a second job offer before you negotiate your raise.
Get a second contractor's quote before you negotiate the renovation.
Know precisely what your house would fetch from a different buyer before you accept a lowball offer.
Your BATNA is not a bluff.
It's homework.

You build it by brainstorming every realistic alternative available to you if this deal falls through, developing the most promising ones into something concrete, and then choosing the single best one as your baseline.
Now compare that to what most people do instead, which is walk in with a fixed bottom line, a hard number decided in advance, I won't sell for under this, I won't accept less than that.
It feels protective, and it's the most common instinct in the world, but the book is fairly blunt about its two big problems.
First, it's arbitrary.

You picked that number before the conversation even started, before you'd heard a single creative idea the other side might bring, which means a bottom line can make you reject a genuinely brilliant deal simply because it doesn't hit your preset figure, even when it actually serves your real interests better than the number ever would have.
Maybe the price is lower than your bottom line, but they're offering owner financing, or a faster close, or they're taking on a repair that would have cost you more than the price gap anyway.
A rigid bottom line can't see any of that, it only sees one number.

Second, a bottom line is too rigid in the other direction too, because it can just as easily let you accept a genuinely bad deal simply because it happens to clear your number, even if your BATNA has quietly improved since you set it and you'd actually be better off walking.
A BATNA doesn't carry either problem.

It isn't an arbitrary line you invented, it's a real, live description of your actual best alternative, and the test it gives you is dynamic and honest, is this offer, right now, in front of me, better or worse than what I would genuinely get by walking away?

That question protects you from a bad deal exactly as well as a bottom line does, but it never stops you from recognizing a great, unconventional deal just because it doesn't resemble the number you scribbled down beforehand.
This is also your whole answer to the question everyone eventually asks, what do I do when the other side is simply more powerful than me, bigger company, more money, more options, more leverage?
You cannot negotiate your way into being bigger than they are.
But you're not actually powerless, because power in a negotiation was never really about size, it's about alternatives.

The single highest impact thing you can do against a more powerful counterpart is spend real effort improving your own BATNA before you ever sit down, because that's the one lever entirely within your control.

And once you're at the table, you protect yourself the same way regardless of who's sitting across from you, by knowing clearly, in your own head, what your walk away point is, grounded in your real BATNA, so you never get talked into a deal that's worse than doing nothing at all, while staying open and creative about every deal that's better than doing nothing, even when it doesn't match the shape you originally imagined.
So what happens when you show up ready to do all of this, people, interests, options, criteria, and the other side just refuses to play along?
They keep hammering positions at you.
They attack, they push, they won't budge.
This happens constantly, and the book has a specific answer for it, called negotiation jujitsu, and the name is deliberate.
In martial arts jujitsu, you don't meet force with force, you don't block a strike with brute strength, you redirect the other person's own momentum past you and let their attack defeat itself.
Negotiation jujitsu works the same way.

When the other side asserts a position hard, your instinct will be to either reject it outright or defend your own position just as hard right back, and both of those responses just cement everyone deeper into a positional fight.
Instead, you sidestep.
You don't attack their position and you don't defend yours, you look past it and ask about the thinking behind it.
What makes that number fair to you?
Help me understand what's driving that deadline.
You're not fighting their position, you're inviting them to explain the interest sitting underneath it, and often the moment you ask sincerely, the temperature in the room drops.
The same move works when they attack your ideas instead of stating their own position.
Don't defend your proposal like it's under siege.
Invite the criticism.
Ask what's wrong with it specifically, ask what they would do differently in your position.
And it works when they attack you personally, which is the hardest version, because every instinct in your body wants to defend yourself or fire back.
Don't.
Let it pass.
Don't treat it as an attack on you as a person, and don't counterattack, because a counterattack just proves to them that attacking works.
Instead, redirect it toward the problem sitting between you.
Two tools carry almost this entire technique.

One is questions instead of statements, because a statement generates resistance, the listener's brain is already building a rebuttal while you're still talking, while a genuine question generates an answer, and often that answer hands you information or an opening a statement never could have.
The other tool is silence, and it's underused precisely because it's uncomfortable.
If you ask a question and the answer feels incomplete, or you've just made a proposal and you're waiting to hear back, stop talking and let the silence sit there.
Most people cannot tolerate silence in a negotiation, and they will fill it, sometimes with exactly the concession or the piece of information you were waiting for.

Sometimes jujitsu alone isn't enough, especially when there are more than two parties involved, or when the relationship between negotiators has broken down so badly that direct back and forth just keeps sparking new fights instead of progress.
For situations like that, the book offers what it calls the one text procedure.

Instead of the two sides trading offers and counteroffers directly, which is exactly the mechanism that drags everyone back into positional bargaining, you bring in a third party, a mediator, sometimes even just one member of one side willing to play this role, and that person's job is not to judge who's right.
Their job is to draft a single proposal, on one page, and circulate it.

Both sides then react to that one document rather than to each other, criticizing it, pointing out what doesn't work for them, without having to formally commit to anything they say, because they're only reacting to a draft, not making an offer.

The mediator revises it based on that feedback, circulates the new version, and the cycle repeats, again and again, the draft slowly absorbing more and more of what both sides actually need, until eventually what's on the page is something everyone can live with.
It sounds almost too gentle to work, but remember that Sinai negotiation we already walked through?
That's literally how it happened.

Over those thirteen days at Camp David in 1978, American mediators drafted and redrafted a single negotiating text more than twenty times, with Begin's team and Sadat's team each reacting to the draft rather than negotiating face to face over an increasingly bitter set of direct demands.
Nobody had to be the one who blinked first, because nobody was making demands of each other directly at all, they were both simply improving the same piece of paper.
That's the one text procedure, and it's worth remembering any time you're stuck in a multi party mess, or a two party fight that has gotten too personal to run through normal back and forth.
Last piece of the toolkit, and it matters, what do you do with someone who isn't just hard, but is actually playing dirty?
The book groups these tactics into three families, and I want you to be able to recognize each one by name, because recognizing a tactic is most of the battle against it.
The first family is deliberate deception, phony facts, someone stating something false with total confidence, hoping you won't check.

Phony authority, someone negotiating with you as if they can say yes, extracting your best offer, and only then revealing they actually need their partner, their board, their spouse to sign off, which conveniently gives them a second round of concessions from you before anyone has actually agreed to anything.
And ambiguous intentions, agreeing to terms while privately planning not to fully honor them.
The second family is psychological warfare, stressful settings, meeting on their turf, uncomfortable chairs, glaring sun, constant interruptions, all designed to wear you down.
Personal attacks, comments about your appearance or your competence, being kept waiting on purpose.

The good guy bad guy routine, one negotiator is harsh and unreasonable, then steps out, and a friendlier negotiator swoops in looking so reasonable by comparison that you're grateful, and far more willing to agree with them, even though their position hasn't actually moved much at all.
And outright threats, which are risky and often escalate a situation rather than resolve it.

The third family is positional pressure tactics, refusing to negotiate at all until you meet some precondition, opening with an extreme, almost insulting demand specifically to shift your sense of what's normal, escalating demands over time so every concession you make gets met with a new ask instead of reciprocation, lock in tactics, someone publicly or contractually boxing themselves in, my hands are tied, my board won't allow it, so that supposedly there's no room left to move, and the
classic take it or leave it ultimatum, presented with no room for discussion at all.
The antidote the book offers is the same three step move no matter which family the tactic comes from.
First, recognize it.

Simply noticing that you're being pressured by a specific, named tactic, rather than experiencing it as vague discomfort or unexplained pressure, strips out a huge amount of its power, because most of these tactics only work on someone who doesn't realize what's happening to them.
Second, raise it explicitly, out loud, calmly, without hostility.
I notice we're negotiating with the sun directly in my eyes, can we switch seats.
It sounds like this is being presented as final, I want to check whether there's actually room to discuss it.
I want to confirm you have the authority to agree to this today, because I do.

Naming a tactic to the person using it is remarkably disarming, because it drags something that only works in the shadows out into the light, and most people, caught being obvious about a manipulation, back off it rather than defend it outright.
Third, negotiate about the negotiation itself.
Once you've named the tactic, don't just absorb it or launch a countertactic of your own, which only escalates things into the exact positional battle you're trying to avoid.

Instead, apply the same principled approach to the process, propose fair ground rules, ask for objective criteria for how you'll proceed, exactly the way you'd apply objective criteria to the substance of the deal itself.
You're not fighting fire with fire.
You're simply refusing to let someone else's manipulation quietly become the actual terms of your agreement.
So let's pull the whole system together, because by now you've got a lot of moving pieces, and I want you to see how they lock into one continuous approach.

You walk into a negotiation and you resist the pull toward positional bargaining, the instinct to plant a number and defend it, whether you're tempted to do that softly, by giving in to protect the relationship, or hard, by digging in and treating it like a fight to win.

Instead, you separate the people from the problem, moving to the same side of the table as the person across from you, dealing openly with perception, emotion, and communication so that ego never gets welded to substance.

You dig underneath positions to find real interests, asking why and why not, remembering that the window only got solved once somebody asked a question instead of proposing another compromise, remembering that Israel and Egypt only found peace once security and sovereignty replaced two immovable lines on a map, remembering the two sisters and their orange, and how an entire negotiation can quietly fail even while both sides believe they're being fair.

You invent options for mutual gain before deciding on anything, catching yourself before premature judgment, before the hunt for a single right answer, before assuming the pie can't grow, before forgetting that solving their problem is actually your fastest path to solving your own.

When interests truly collide and somebody has to move, you insist on objective criteria, market value, expert opinion, precedent, what a court would decide, so the resolution comes from fairness rather than from whoever is willing to be more stubborn.

Underneath all of it, you know your BATNA cold, you keep improving it before and during every important negotiation, and you use it, not a rigid bottom line you invented in advance, as your real test for whether to walk away.

And when the other side won't meet you there, when they push positions or play dirty, you don't match their force, you sidestep with negotiation jujitsu, you ask questions, you sit in silence, and when things are truly stuck, you reach for a one text procedure, letting a single evolving draft absorb everyone's concerns instead of trading demands back and forth.

Whenever you spot a specific tactic, phony authority, good guy bad guy, an extreme opening demand, a take it or leave it ultimatum, you name it out loud, calmly, and you negotiate about the rules of the game itself, not just the outcome.
So here's what I want you to actually do with all of this, starting this week, because a book like this is wasted if it just becomes a pleasant memory of something you listened to in the car.
Pick one real negotiation sitting in your life right now, today, not a hypothetical one.
Maybe it's a raise you've been meaning to ask for, a big purchase coming up, a disagreement with a family member that keeps almost happening and then getting swallowed.
Pick one, and run it through this book on purpose.
First, before you say a single word to the other side, sit down and write out your BATNA.
Not a vague feeling that you have options, an actual, specific, honest answer to the question, what will I really do if this falls through.

If your BATNA is weak, spend this week strengthening it before you do anything else, get that second quote, have that other conversation, line up the alternative, because that work alone will do more for your leverage than any clever line you could say at the table.
Second, write down your own position, whatever you'd currently say you want, and then force yourself to write down the interest underneath it, the real why.

Then do the harder version of that same exercise for the other person, write down what you think their position will be, and then really sit with why they might actually hold it, what fear or need or hope is likely driving them.
You'll probably find you're guessing on that second one, and that's fine, that's exactly what your first real conversation with them is for.
Third, before you meet, spend fifteen minutes actually brainstorming options, alone or with someone you trust, without judging a single idea while you're generating it.
Aim for more options than feels reasonable.

You're looking for the version of the orange and the peel hiding somewhere in your specific situation, the move that gives both sides more than either of you expected, and you won't find it if the first idea you have is also the last one you consider.
Fourth, go find your objective criteria before you need them.
If it's a salary, pull real market data for your role and your area.
If it's a purchase, get real comparables.
Walk in with a fair, external standard already in your pocket, so if things get stuck, you have something better to reach for than just repeating your number louder.
And fifth, promise yourself one specific behavior change for this exact conversation.
The next time you feel the pull to defend your position, or to fire back at an attack, ask a question instead, and then let yourself sit in the silence that follows it.

That one habit, question and silence, question and silence, is worth more than almost anything else in this entire book, and it's the easiest one to start practicing today, on a conversation that has nothing to do with any deal at all, just to feel how it works before you actually need it.
Here's what I want you to carry out of this car, or off this treadmill, more than any single technique.
The people who wrote this book didn't set out to teach you how to win arguments.

They set out to teach you how to stop treating every disagreement as a fight with a winner and a loser, because most of the disagreements in your actual life, with your spouse, your business partner, your kid, your neighbor, aren't one time transactions you walk away from, they're relationships you have to keep living inside of long after the deal is done.

Getting to Yes is really an argument that you don't have to choose between being kind and being effective, that the two were never actually in tension the way soft and hard bargaining make them look, that you can be soft on the people and hard on the problem, all at once, every single time, a phrase this book made famous for good reason.
So the next time you feel a negotiation sliding toward a standoff, a number against a number, a position against a position, stop, and ask why.
Find the interest hiding under the demand.
Look for the option nobody's mentioned yet.
Reach for a standard that's fair to everyone in the room, not just you.
Know exactly what you'll do if you walk away, and let that knowledge make you calm instead of desperate.
That's the whole method.
Now go have the conversation you've been putting off, and go get to yes.
